assertion_results.text: The plan explicitly references reusing common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter for comma-separated multi-value query parameter parsing rather than writing new parsing logic (constraint 5.4)
assertion_results.evidence: The plan explicitly references apply_filter multiple times. In the inspection step: "common/src/db/query.rs -- read the apply_filter function signature and behavior. Confirm it accepts a comma-separated string, splits it, and generates a SQL IN clause. This is the function we will reuse directly for parsing and SQL generation." In the service modification step: "Import and use apply_filter from common/src/db/query.rs: This is the critical reuse point. apply_filter already handles: Splitting a comma-separated string into individual values, Generating a SQL WHERE ... IN (...) clause, Handling the single-value case (no comma) as a simple equality check." The plan also explicitly states: "No new utility functions: Do NOT create any new parsing or filtering helper."

assertion_results.text: The plan references following the filter pattern from modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs as a structural guide for implementing the license filter
assertion_results.evidence: The plan references the advisory list endpoint as a structural template throughout. In the inspection step: "modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs -- the severity filter implementation here is the structural template." In the Query struct modification: "Following the exact pattern from modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs, add an Option<String> field named license" and "The advisory endpoint's Query struct has an optional severity field that follows this same pattern -- mirror it exactly." In the handler modification: "Follow the same call pattern used in the advisory list handler when it passes query.severity to AdvisoryService::list()." In the service modification: "mirroring how AdvisoryService::list accepts an optional severity parameter."

assertion_results.text: The plan references using entity/src/package_license.rs for the JOIN query rather than writing raw SQL or creating a new entity
assertion_results.evidence: The plan clearly references using the existing entity for JOIN queries. In the inspection step: "entity/src/package_license.rs -- inspect the SeaORM entity to understand the table schema (columns, relations) for the package-license join table. This entity will be used in the JOIN query." In the service modification: "Add a JOIN through entity/src/package_license.rs: Use the SeaORM entity package_license::Entity to construct a JOIN... use its Relation definitions to build the join rather than writing raw SQL." The code example shows: "query.join(JoinType::InnerJoin, package_license::Relation::Package.def().rev())". The out-of-scope section confirms: "entity/src/package_license.rs -- the entity is used for JOIN queries but not modified."

assertion_results.text: The reuse analysis covers all 3 Reuse Candidates from the task description, explaining how each would be applied
assertion_results.evidence: The reuse-analysis.md covers all three candidates with dedicated sections, each containing "What it provides" and "How it is reused" subsections. Reuse Candidate 1: apply_filter from common/src/db/query.rs -- explains it is called directly in PackageService::list for comma-separated parsing and SQL generation. Reuse Candidate 2: advisory/endpoints/list.rs severity filter -- explains it is used as a structural pattern, mirrored step-by-step for the Query struct, handler wiring, and service-level filter application. Reuse Candidate 3: entity/src/package_license.rs -- explains the entity is used for type-safe JOIN construction via SeaORM relations. The summary states: "All three Reuse Candidates identified in the task description are used." A summary table maps each candidate to its usage type and location.

assertion_results.text: All files listed in the plan are within the scope defined by the task's Files to Modify and Files to Create sections (constraint 5.1)
assertion_results.evidence: The plan's "Files to Modify" section (step 2) lists: modules/fundamental/src/package/endpoints/list.rs and modules/fundamental/src/package/service/mod.rs. The "Files to Create" section (step 3) lists: tests/api/package_license_filter.rs. The plan includes an explicit "Files NOT modified (out of scope)" section (step 4) listing files that are intentionally left unchanged: package/endpoints/mod.rs, package/model/summary.rs, common/src/db/query.rs, entity/src/package_license.rs, common/src/model/paginated.rs. The plan also mentions docs/api.md in a separate "Documentation impact" section (step 7) and tests/api/mod.rs for module registration, which are reasonable supporting changes that would typically be in scope for a feature implementation. The plan demonstrates scope discipline throughout.

assertion_results.text: The plan does not propose creating new utility functions that duplicate the functionality of apply_filter (constraint 5.4)
assertion_results.evidence: The plan explicitly states in the endpoint modification section: "No new utility functions: Do NOT create any new parsing or filtering helper. The comma-separated string is passed as-is to the service layer, which delegates to apply_filter from common/src/db/query.rs." The reuse analysis reinforces this with a dedicated section "New code that is NOT created (duplication avoidance)" listing: "No parse_comma_separated() function -- apply_filter in common/src/db/query.rs already handles comma-separated parsing internally. No build_license_filter() function -- apply_filter already generates the SQL condition (equality or IN clause) based on the input. No custom LicenseFilter struct -- the Option<String> pattern used by the advisory severity filter is sufficient."
